architecture.summary: This is a benchmark paper, not a model architecture paper. It compares three LLM output generation approaches: (1) plain JSON generation via standard sampling, (2) JSON with constrained decoding (structured output), and (3) TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) format with one-shot in-context learning. Information flows from LLM completion → validation pipeline → repair loop (up to 3 cycles) with error text re-injected as context. The paper evaluates 21 models across 4 test cases (users, order, company, invoice) measuring one-shot accuracy, final accuracy, and token consumption.

biblio.title: Token-Oriented Object Notation vs JSON: A Benchmark of Plain and Constrained Decoding Generation

claims.statement: TOON shows promising accuracy/token consumption ratio for in-domain generation tasks, though this advantage is often reduced by the 'prompt tax' of instructional overhead in shorter contexts.
For TOON-aligned cases (users, order, invoice), TOON achieved 90.5% one-shot accuracy in the users case while using 22% fewer tokens than plain JSON.
In the users case, JSON-SO (Structured Output) proved to be the absolute token efficiency winner at 556 tokens vs TOON 840 tokens.

claims.dataset: benchmark (aggregated across users, order, company, invoice)
claims.statement: NousResearch/Hermes-4-405B dropped from 92.5% one-shot accuracy in plain JSON to 35.0% in JSON-SO (constrained decoding).

claims.dataset: benchmark (aggregated across users, order, company, invoice)
claims.statement: For Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-fast, plain JSON generation achieved 0% accuracy consuming 37,705 tokens, while JSON-SO achieved 75% accuracy with 4,440 tokens.
In the company case (non-aligned scenario with deep nesting), TOON achieved 0% one-shot accuracy, while JSON achieved 18.6% and JSON-SO achieved 21.9%.
TOON's true efficiency potential likely follows a non-linear curve, shining only beyond a specific point where cumulative syntax savings of large datasets amortize the initial prompt overhead.
For simple structures, constrained decoding (JSON-SO) outperformed TOON, hinting that TOON enforcing via frameworks such as xgrammar may not yield the desired results.
